description: HIPAA is actually several rules, not one: the Privacy Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart E), the Security Rule (Subpart C), and the Breach Notification Rule (Subpart D) — plus an Enforcement Rule covering how OCR investigates and penalizes violations. Unlike SOC 2, this is a federal legal requirement, not a voluntary attestation — it applies to covered entities and their business associates.

source.label: HHS.gov — Summary of the HIPAA Security Rule

items: HIPAA — Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (1996), specifically its Security Rule (finalized 2003) and Privacy Rule.
A U.S. federal law, not a voluntary standard — non-compliance carries real legal penalties, enforced by HHS's Office for Civil Rights (OCR).

Applies to **covered entities** (health plans, healthcare clearinghouses, and healthcare providers who transmit health information electronically) and their **business associates** (any vendor that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI on a covered entity's behalf).

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

blocks.text: HIPAA vs SOC 2

items: **HIPAA** — legally mandatory once you touch protected health information (PHI); enforced by OCR with statutory penalties.
**SOC 2** — voluntary, industry-agnostic; a SOC 2 report doesn't satisfy HIPAA's legal requirements on its own.

blocks.text: See the [SOC 2 vs HIPAA comparison](/frameworks/soc2) on the SOC 2 page for the reverse framing — many healthcare-adjacent SaaS companies pursue both, since HIPAA is the legal floor and SOC 2 is the customer-facing trust signal on top of it.
Required vs Addressable Specifications

items: **Required** — you must implement the specification exactly as written. No flexibility.

**Addressable** — you must assess whether the specification is reasonable and appropriate for your organization, and either implement it, implement an equivalent alternative, or document why it isn't applicable. "Addressable" does not mean "optional" — you still need a documented decision either way.

blocks.text: This required/addressable split is unique to HIPAA among the frameworks here — SOC 2 and ISO 27001 don't have an equivalent "document why you skipped it" escape hatch built into the standard itself.

items: **Privacy Rule** (§164.500–534) — governs use and disclosure of PHI in any form (not just electronic), and the specific rights individuals have over their own health information: access, amendment, accounting of disclosures, restrictions, and notice.

**Security Rule** (§164.302–318) — the Administrative, Physical, and Technical Safeguards for *electronic* PHI (ePHI) specifically. This is the part most people mean when they say "HIPAA compliance," but it's only one of the rules.
**Breach Notification Rule** (§164.400–414) — added by HITECH; requires notifying individuals, HHS, and sometimes the media following a breach of unsecured PHI.
**Enforcement Rule** (Part 160, Subparts C–E) — how OCR actually investigates complaints and levies penalties across all of the above.

**State law** — HIPAA sets a federal floor, not a ceiling. More protective state health-privacy laws (e.g. California's CMIA, Texas HB 300) still apply on top of it and aren't preempted — "we're HIPAA compliant" doesn't automatically mean state requirements are covered too.

blocks.text: Unlike SOC 2's optional Trust Services Categories, none of these HIPAA rules are optional — there's no equivalent to "Security is required, the rest is optional." What varies within the Security Rule specifically is which implementation specifications are required vs. addressable (see Key Differences above).
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/hipaa/controls) for the full breakdown — 18 Security Rule standards, 8 Privacy Rule provisions, and 5 Breach Notification Rule requirements.
It's Not Optional

items: **Legal requirement** — if you're a covered entity or business associate handling PHI, HIPAA compliance isn't a business choice.
**Real penalties** — OCR can levy civil penalties per violation (tiered by culpability), and criminal charges are possible for willful neglect or intentional misuse.
**Breach notification obligations** — a PHI breach triggers mandatory notification to affected individuals, HHS, and in some cases the media, under the HITECH Breach Notification Rule.

blocks.text: Business Reasons Beyond the Legal Floor

items: **Trust with healthcare customers** — covered entities won't sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with a vendor that can't demonstrate a real HIPAA security program.
**Reduced breach risk** — healthcare data breaches are consistently among the most expensive per-record of any industry, per IBM's Cost of a Data Breach Report.

blocks.text: Quick self-score across the Security, Privacy, and Breach Notification Rules. If you're answering "no" to more than half the questions in any category, prioritize that category first.

rows: Have you conducted a formal, documented risk analysis covering all ePHI?
Is there a named individual responsible for your security program?
Do employees get role-based access and HIPAA-specific security training?
Is access to ePHI granted/revoked through a formal authorization process?
Do you have a documented process for responding to and reporting security incidents?
Do you have a tested data backup, disaster recovery, and emergency mode operation plan?
Do you have signed BAAs with every vendor that touches ePHI?
Is physical access to areas storing ePHI restricted and logged?
Are workstations that access ePHI physically secured (e.g. screen locks, positioning)?
Do you have a documented process for wiping/destroying media before disposal or reuse?
Does every user have a unique ID, with automatic logoff and encryption where appropriate?
Do you log and review access to systems containing ePHI?
Do you have mechanisms to detect improper alteration or destruction of ePHI?
Do you verify the identity of anyone requesting access to ePHI?
Is ePHI encrypted in transit whenever it crosses a network?
Can you fulfill a right-of-access request within 30 days?
Is your NPP current and does it match your actual data practices?
Do staff access only the PHI necessary for their specific role?
Do you have a documented process to assess whether an incident is a reportable breach?
Could you notify affected individuals and HHS within 60 days of discovery today?

blocks.text: Identifying Gaps

items: The risk analysis (Administrative Safeguard §164.308(a)(1)) is the foundational document — nearly everything else in a HIPAA program traces back to gaps it identifies.
[HHS's Security Rule Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/guidance/index.html) is the canonical resource for scoping this yourself.

blocks.text: What Takes Longest

The risk analysis and remediation cycle is usually the long pole — you can't meaningfully start Administrative Safeguard work until the risk analysis identifies what's actually missing, and fixing findings (especially contingency planning and business associate agreement cleanup across every vendor) tends to stretch out over months.
What to Implement First

items: **A formal risk analysis** — everything else depends on it, and OCR treats a missing or superficial risk analysis as one of the most common audit findings.
**Assign a named security official** — required, cheap, and unblocks accountability for everything after it.
**Unique user IDs and access logging** — usually a configuration change in systems you already run.
**Business Associate Agreements** — an inventory-and-paperwork exercise, but one that's easy to let slip and expensive to fix retroactively after a breach.
Once those are in place, move to the higher-effort items — contingency planning (backup/DR/emergency mode) and physical facility controls — since those need real testing, not just documentation.

blocks.text: Unlike SOC 2, there's no fixed "observation period" — HIPAA compliance is a continuous program, not a point-in-time report. A first-time implementation typically takes 2–6 months to reach a reasonable baseline.
Step-by-Step Process

items: **Scope** — inventory every system, vendor, and workflow that touches ePHI.
**Risk analysis** — the formal, documented assessment required under §164.308(a)(1).
**Remediation** — implement fixes for identified gaps across all three safeguard categories.
**Policies & documentation** — write (or update) the policies §164.316 requires, and retain them for 6 years.
**Training rollout** — get the whole workforce through security awareness training, documented.
**Ongoing monitoring** — this isn't a one-time project; risk analysis and control review need to recur.

blocks.text: HIPAA doesn't have a voluntary third-party audit like SOC 2's CPA-issued report. Compliance is typically demonstrated internally (via your risk analysis and documentation) and tested externally only when OCR investigates — usually triggered by a breach report or a complaint.
How Enforcement Actually Happens

items: **Breach-triggered** — a reportable breach (500+ affected individuals) is publicly listed on HHS's "Wall of Shame" and often triggers an OCR investigation.
**Complaint-triggered** — patients or employees can file complaints directly with OCR.
**Periodic OCR audits** — HHS runs occasional audit programs of covered entities and business associates, though these are far less routine than SOC 2's annual audit cycle.

blocks.text: Common Findings

OCR's enforcement history consistently points to the same handful of gaps: no (or an inadequate) risk analysis, missing encryption on portable devices, insufficient access controls, missing or incomplete Business Associate Agreements — and, on the Privacy Rule side, delayed responses to patient right-of-access requests, which OCR made a specific, standalone enforcement priority.
Compliance is ongoing — there's no annual report to renew like SOC 2, but the risk analysis and workforce training need to happen on a recurring basis regardless.
Ongoing Requirements

Re-run the risk analysis whenever your systems, vendors, or workflows change materially, and at minimum annually. Refresh workforce training annually. Review and update policies as regulations or your environment change.
Scaling Compliance
As you add new systems or vendors that touch ePHI, they need to be brought into scope for the risk analysis and get a signed BAA before they go live — not after.

categories.title: Administrative Safeguards
categories.summary: Policies, processes, and workforce management governing how ePHI is protected — the largest of the three safeguard categories (45 CFR §164.308).

controls.criteria: Security Management Process (§164.308(a)(1)) — Required: Risk Analysis, Risk Management, Sanction Policy, Information System Activity Review.
controls.notes: The foundational control everything else traces back to. A documented, org-wide risk analysis is non-negotiable — OCR flags a missing or superficial one in nearly every enforcement action.

controls.criteria: Assigned Security Responsibility (§164.308(a)(2)) — Required: identify the security official responsible for developing and implementing security policies.
controls.notes: Name one person. It can be a part-time responsibility at a small org, but it needs to be someone specific, not "the team."

controls.criteria: Workforce Security (§164.308(a)(3)) — Addressable: Authorization/Supervision, Workforce Clearance Procedure, Termination Procedures.
controls.notes: Role-based access provisioning, background-check policy for roles touching ePHI, and a documented, prompt offboarding process tied to HR.

controls.criteria: Information Access Management (§164.308(a)(4)) — Required (if applicable): Isolating Healthcare Clearinghouse Functions. Addressable: Access Authorization, Access Establishment and Modification.
controls.notes: A formal request/approval workflow for granting or changing access to ePHI, with an audit trail.

controls.criteria: Security Awareness and Training (§164.308(a)(5)) — Addressable: Security Reminders, Protection from Malicious Software, Log-in Monitoring, Password Management.
controls.notes: Annual, documented training for the whole workforce — not just IT. Track completion.

controls.criteria: Security Incident Procedures (§164.308(a)(6)) — Required: Response and Reporting.
controls.notes: A written incident response plan with defined reporting steps, tied into the HITECH breach notification timeline.

controls.criteria: Contingency Plan (§164.308(a)(7)) — Required: Data Backup Plan, Disaster Recovery Plan, Emergency Mode Operation Plan. Addressable: Testing and Revision Procedures, Applications and Data Criticality Analysis.
controls.notes: Automated backups, a tested DR plan, and a plan for operating (even in a degraded state) during an emergency. "Addressable" testing still needs to actually happen and be documented.

controls.criteria: Evaluation (§164.308(a)(8)) — Required: periodic technical and non-technical evaluation of security safeguards.
controls.notes: A recurring (at least annual) internal review of whether your controls still hold up — especially after any environment change.

controls.criteria: Business Associate Contracts and Other Arrangements (§164.308(b)(1)) — Required: written contract or arrangement with satisfactory assurances.
controls.notes: A signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with every vendor that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI on your behalf — before they go live, not after.

categories.title: Physical Safeguards
categories.summary: Controlling physical access to the facilities and devices where ePHI lives (45 CFR §164.310).

controls.criteria: Facility Access Controls (§164.310(a)(1)) — Addressable: Contingency Operations, Facility Security Plan, Access Control and Validation Procedures, Maintenance Records.
controls.notes: Badge access or equivalent for spaces housing ePHI systems, with a plan for facility access during emergencies.

controls.criteria: Workstation Use (§164.310(b)) — Required: policies specifying the proper functions and manner of use for workstations that access ePHI.
controls.notes: A written acceptable-use policy — what workstations can be used for, and how, when they touch ePHI.

controls.criteria: Workstation Security (§164.310(c)) — Required: physical safeguards for all workstations accessing ePHI, restricting access to authorized users.
controls.notes: Screen locks, physical positioning away from public view, and restricted physical access to the devices themselves.

controls.criteria: Device and Media Controls (§164.310(d)(1)) — Required: Disposal, Media Re-use. Addressable: Accountability, Data Backup and Storage.
controls.notes: A documented process for wiping or destroying media before disposal or reuse, and tracking hardware that stores ePHI.

categories.title: Technical Safeguards
categories.summary: The technology controls enforcing access and protecting ePHI within the systems themselves (45 CFR §164.312).

controls.criteria: Access Control (§164.312(a)(1)) — Required: Unique User Identification, Emergency Access Procedure. Addressable: Automatic Logoff, Encryption and Decryption.
controls.notes: Every user gets a unique login (no shared accounts), plus a documented way to get emergency access to ePHI if normal access breaks.

controls.criteria: Audit Controls (§164.312(b)) — Required: hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms to record and examine activity in systems with ePHI.
controls.notes: Logging on every system that touches ePHI, with someone actually reviewing those logs, not just collecting them.

controls.criteria: Integrity (§164.312(c)(1)) — Addressable: mechanisms to authenticate ePHI and confirm it hasn't been improperly altered or destroyed.
controls.notes: Checksums, versioning, or audit trails that let you detect unauthorized changes to patient data.

controls.criteria: Person or Entity Authentication (§164.312(d)) — Required: verify that a person or entity seeking access to ePHI is who they claim to be.
controls.notes: MFA is the practical modern implementation of this requirement, even though the rule text predates it.

controls.criteria: Transmission Security (§164.312(e)(1)) — Addressable: Integrity Controls, Encryption.
controls.notes: TLS (or equivalent) for any ePHI crossing a network — email, APIs, file transfers. "Addressable" here is close to "required" in practice; there's rarely a defensible reason not to encrypt ePHI in transit.

categories.title: Privacy Rule
categories.summary: Governs use and disclosure of PHI generally (not just electronic), and the specific rights individuals have over their own health information (45 CFR §164.500–534).

controls.criteria: Uses and disclosures of PHI (§164.502) — a covered entity may not use or disclose PHI except as the Privacy Rule permits or requires, and must apply the minimum necessary standard except for treatment, when disclosing to the individual, or when authorized.
controls.notes: The baseline rule everything else follows from: no PHI use/disclosure without a Privacy-Rule-recognized reason, and even then, only the minimum necessary for the purpose.

controls.criteria: Authorization (§164.508) — uses/disclosures not otherwise permitted by the Privacy Rule (e.g. most marketing, sale of PHI) require a valid, specific written authorization from the individual.
controls.notes: A generic consent form signed at intake does not cover this — authorization has specific required elements and needs to name the actual use/disclosure.

controls.criteria: Notice of Privacy Practices (§164.520) — covered entities must provide individuals a plain-language notice describing how PHI may be used/disclosed, the individual's rights, and the entity's legal duties.
controls.notes: Post it, hand it out at first service delivery, and make sure it actually reflects your real practices — a boilerplate NPP that doesn't match reality is a common audit finding.

controls.criteria: Right to request restrictions and confidential communications (§164.522) — individuals can request limits on certain uses/disclosures, and covered entities must agree in specific cases (e.g. a self-pay item disclosed to a health plan).
controls.notes: Build a process to receive, evaluate, and honor (or document declining) restriction requests — the self-pay case is not discretionary.

controls.criteria: Right of access (§164.524) — individuals can inspect and obtain a copy of their PHI in a designated record set, generally within 30 days.
controls.notes: One of the most frequently enforced rights — OCR has run entire initiatives specifically targeting access-request delays. Have a defined, fast turnaround process.

controls.criteria: Right to amend (§164.526) — individuals can request correction of PHI they believe is inaccurate or incomplete, with the covered entity required to act within 60 days.
controls.notes: You can deny an amendment request in specific circumstances, but you need a documented process either way, including how accepted amendments get communicated to others who received the original data.

controls.criteria: Right to an accounting of disclosures (§164.528) — individuals can request a list of certain disclosures of their PHI made in the prior six years.
controls.notes: Excludes routine treatment/payment/operations disclosures — but you need to actually track the disclosures that do count, which most systems don't log by default.

controls.criteria: Administrative requirements (§164.530) — designate a privacy official, train the workforce, apply appropriate safeguards, and provide a process for complaints.
controls.notes: The Privacy Rule's own governance layer — distinct from (but overlapping with) the Security Rule's Administrative Safeguards. Needs its own named owner, not an assumption that the Security Officer covers it.

categories.title: Breach Notification Rule
categories.summary: Requires notification following a breach of unsecured PHI — to individuals, HHS, and in some cases the media (45 CFR §164.400–414, added by HITECH).

controls.criteria: Breach definition and risk assessment (§164.400–402) — an impermissible use/disclosure of PHI is presumed to be a breach unless a documented risk assessment shows a low probability the data was compromised.
controls.notes: "We don't think anyone actually looked at it" isn't sufficient — the risk assessment needs to walk through specific factors (nature of the data, who accessed it, whether it was actually viewed/acquired, mitigation) and be documented.

controls.criteria: Notification to individuals (§164.404) — without unreasonable delay, and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery of the breach.
controls.notes: The clock starts at discovery, not confirmation — a suspected breach under investigation still counts as "discovered" for timing purposes in most cases.

controls.criteria: Notification to media (§164.406) — required in addition to individual notification when a breach affects more than 500 residents of a single state or jurisdiction, within the same 60-day window.
controls.notes: Only triggered at the 500-in-one-state threshold — most breaches don't reach this, but it needs to be checked, not assumed away.

controls.criteria: Notification to the HHS Secretary (§164.408) — breaches affecting 500+ individuals require notification to HHS at the same time as individual notification; breaches affecting fewer than 500 can be logged and reported annually, within 60 days of year-end.
controls.notes: Track every breach regardless of size — even sub-500 incidents need to go into the annual log, not just the big ones.

controls.criteria: Notification by business associates (§164.410) — a business associate that discovers a breach must notify the covered entity without unreasonable delay, no later than 60 calendar days after discovery.
controls.notes: Your BA agreements should specify a notification timeline tighter than the statutory 60 days — you still need enough runway to meet your own individual/HHS notification deadlines after they tell you.

blocks.text: The Overview and Controls tabs are a reference and orientation layer — an accurate map of what HIPAA requires and why, with prioritization guidance. They are not, on their own, enough to implement a compliance program or make a company HIPAA compliant.

What's missing from a reference page by nature: an org-specific risk analysis (the one artifact almost everything else depends on), actual policy/procedure documents, legal review of BAAs and any state law layered on top of HIPAA, technical implementation tied to your real infrastructure, and someone with authority actually running the program day to day.
This tab exists to close part of that gap — a lightweight methodology outline, links to HHS's own free tools, and a concrete list of what you'll actually need to produce.
HIPAA doesn't mandate a specific risk analysis methodology, but OCR frequently points to NIST SP 800-30 as an acceptable approach. A minimally sufficient risk analysis walks through:

items: **Scope it** — identify every system, application, and location where ePHI is created, received, maintained, or transmitted. This is your asset inventory; nothing after this step is more important.

**Identify threats and vulnerabilities** — for each asset, what could go wrong (threat) and what weakness would let it happen (vulnerability)? Don't just copy a generic list — it needs to reflect your actual environment.
**Assess current controls** — what's already in place mitigating each threat/vulnerability pair?
**Determine likelihood and impact** — rate each risk, even on a simple High/Medium/Low scale, consistently applied.
**Determine overall risk level** — likelihood × impact, producing a prioritized risk list, not just a flat inventory.

**Document findings and a remediation plan** — this document is what OCR asks for first in almost every investigation. "We did an analysis but didn't write it down" is treated the same as not having done one.

blocks.text: Re-run this whenever systems, vendors, or workflows change materially — and at minimum annually even without a specific trigger. A three-year-old risk analysis that's never been revisited is one of the most common OCR findings.

items: [HHS/ONC Security Risk Assessment (SRA) Tool](https://www.healthit.gov/topic/privacy-security-and-hipaa/security-risk-assessment-tool) — a free, downloadable tool (Windows app or Excel workbook) that walks through the risk analysis process step by step. Built specifically for small/medium practices, but the structure is useful at any size. Nothing entered into it is collected or transmitted to HHS.

[HHS Sample Business Associate Agreement Provisions](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/covered-entities/sample-business-associate-agreement-provisions/index.html) — official sample BAA language covering permitted uses, safeguards, breach reporting, and subcontractor flow-down. Explicitly not required verbatim, but a solid starting point before legal review.

[HHS Security Rule Guidance Material](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/guidance/index.html) — HHS's own implementation guidance for each Security Rule standard, one level more detailed than this site's summaries.

resources.title: Artifacts You'll Actually Need to Produce

blocks.text: A checklist of the concrete documents a real program needs — this site tells you why each is required; you (or counsel, for the contract-language ones) still have to write them.

items: Risk analysis and risk management plan (the foundational documents — see the methodology above)
Written policies and procedures for every Administrative Safeguard standard, not just a single "security policy" document
Sanction policy for workforce members who violate policy
Notice of Privacy Practices, reviewed against what you actually do with PHI
Signed Business Associate Agreements with every vendor touching PHI
Incident response plan, tabletop-tested with a record that the test happened
Contingency plan: backup, disaster recovery, and emergency mode operation procedures
Workforce training materials and a completion log
Breach risk-assessment template and a notification runbook that can hit the 60-day clock

blocks.text: A realistic starting list of the actual named policies/plans a HIPAA program needs — the common abbreviations are included since you'll see them used interchangeably in audits and vendor questionnaires.

rows: The top-level policy governing the whole security program — everything else hangs off this.
Methodology and cadence for identifying, scoring, and treating risk to ePHI.
How access to ePHI is requested, approved, reviewed, and revoked.
Screening, role-based access, and consequences for policy violations.
Training content, cadence, and completion tracking for the workforce.
Detection, containment, eradication, and recovery steps for a security incident, with defined roles.
Data backup, disaster recovery, and emergency-mode operation procedures, with a testing cadence.
Handling, tracking, and sanitizing media/devices that touch ePHI, including disposal.
Physical access controls for spaces housing ePHI systems.
What gets logged, retention period, and who reviews logs on what cadence.
When and how encryption is applied to ePHI at rest and in transit.
Vendor risk assessment, BAA execution, and ongoing oversight of business associates.
The risk-assessment methodology and notification runbook for a suspected breach.
The patient-facing notice describing how PHI is used, disclosed, and how to exercise rights.
Rules limiting PHI access/disclosure to the minimum needed for the purpose.
Process and timelines for fulfilling access, amendment, and accounting-of-disclosures requests.
How long PHI/ePHI is kept and how it's destroyed when retention ends.

blocks.text: This isn't an exhaustive list, and smaller orgs often combine several of these into fewer documents (e.g. one "Security Policies and Procedures" binder covering ISP/RA/ACP/SAT together). What matters to OCR is that each requirement is actually addressed in writing and followed — not that you have exactly this many separate files.

resources.title: When to Bring in Outside Help

items: **Legal review** — before finalizing BAA language, and to check whether state law (e.g. California's CMIA, Texas HB 300) adds requirements beyond federal HIPAA in states where you operate.
**A HIPAA compliance consultant** — if this is your first program build, or you lack in-house security/compliance staff to own the risk analysis and ongoing monitoring.
**Outside counsel or a breach coach** — immediately upon discovering a suspected breach, given the tight 60-day notification clock and the legal judgment involved in the required risk assessment.

blocks.text: None of this replaces professional advice specific to your organization — treat everything on this site as a map, not a substitute for the territory.
